blocks.content: He was awake before his alarm.

He sat on the edge of the bed in the early morning light and did nothing for a while. Not scrolling, not moving — just sitting with the quality of the day, which had a different texture than other days even before anything had happened in it. The light through his window was the May light, which was generous. He'd been trying to paint that specific quality of it for weeks and hadn't landed it.
He got up. Showered. Stood in front of his closet longer than the decision warranted.
He chose the blue shirt. He didn't explicitly think about why. He just chose it.

He made coffee carefully — the measurement precise, the water at the right temperature, the cup warmed first. He did everything this morning the way he did things when they mattered, which was the same way he did things when they didn't matter, except that he was aware of it.

He sat at the kitchen table with the coffee and his sketchbook and opened to the drawing he'd made of her on the porch — the one from that night after the Copper Mug when he'd gone home and drawn her from memory, her figure at the edge of the porch railing, looking at something outside the frame.
He looked at it for a long time.

He hadn't tried to draw what she was looking at. He'd left the edge of the page open — the thing outside the border, unknown, unnamed. He'd thought at the time it was the most honest drawing he'd done in months.
He thought now that today he would find out what was in that space.
He finished his coffee. He washed the cup. He texted Nathan: On my way.
